questions.question: What is a subquery?
options: A query that runs on a separate database
A query nested inside another query
A query that only returns one column
A query that modifies table structure
questions.answer: A query nested inside another query

questions.question: What is a scalar subquery?
options: A subquery that returns multiple rows and columns
A subquery that returns a single value
A subquery that modifies data
A subquery that creates a new table
questions.answer: A subquery that returns a single value

questions.question: Which SQL clause is most commonly used to place a subquery that filters rows?
options: SELECT
FROM
WHERE
GROUP BY
questions.answer: WHERE

questions.question:
What does the following query return?
SELECT name FROM students WHERE gpa > (SELECT AVG(gpa) FROM students);
options: All students
Students with GPA above the overall average
The student with the highest GPA
Students with GPA below the overall average
questions.answer: Students with GPA above the overall average

questions.question: Which keyword is used with a subquery that returns a list of values?
options: BETWEEN
LIKE
IN
HAVING
questions.answer: IN

questions.question: What does NOT IN do when used with a subquery?
options: Returns rows where the value matches any result from the subquery
Returns rows where the value does not match any result from the subquery
Returns rows where the subquery returns no results
Deletes rows that match the subquery results
questions.answer: Returns rows where the value does not match any result from the subquery

questions.question: What does the EXISTS keyword check?
options: Whether a table exists in the database
Whether a column contains NULL values
Whether a subquery returns any rows at all
Whether a view has been created
questions.answer: Whether a subquery returns any rows at all

questions.question: What value is commonly selected inside an EXISTS subquery?
options: SELECT *
SELECT COUNT(*)
SELECT 1
SELECT NULL
questions.answer: SELECT 1

questions.question: What is a derived table?
options: A table created with CREATE TABLE
A subquery placed in the FROM clause that acts as a temporary table
A table that stores derived calculations permanently
A table that references another database
questions.answer: A subquery placed in the FROM clause that acts as a temporary table

questions.question: When using a subquery in the FROM clause, what must you provide?
options: A WHERE clause
An alias for the derived table
A LIMIT clause
A PRIMARY KEY
questions.answer: An alias for the derived table

questions.question: What is a correlated subquery?
options: A subquery that runs independently of the outer query
A subquery that references a column from the outer query
A subquery that returns exactly two columns
A subquery that uses only aggregate functions
questions.answer: A subquery that references a column from the outer query

questions.question: How many times does a correlated subquery execute?
options: Once for the entire query
Once per table in the database
Once for each row processed by the outer query
Twice: once for filtering and once for sorting
questions.answer: Once for each row processed by the outer query

questions.question: What is a view in SQL?
options: A copy of a table stored in memory
A saved query that acts like a virtual table
A temporary table that persists between sessions
A backup of the database schema
questions.answer: A saved query that acts like a virtual table

questions.question: Which statement creates a view?
options: MAKE VIEW view_name AS SELECT ...
CREATE VIEW view_name AS SELECT ...
BUILD VIEW view_name FROM SELECT ...
DEFINE VIEW view_name AS SELECT ...
questions.answer: CREATE VIEW view_name AS SELECT ...

questions.question: Does a view store data by itself?
options: Yes, it stores a snapshot of the data at creation time
Yes, it duplicates the data from the underlying tables
No, it runs the stored query each time it is accessed
No, but it caches results after the first query
questions.answer: No, it runs the stored query each time it is accessed

questions.question: How do you query a view after it has been created?
options: EXECUTE VIEW view_name
RUN view_name
SELECT * FROM view_name
CALL view_name
questions.answer: SELECT * FROM view_name

questions.question: Which statement removes a view from the database?
options: DELETE VIEW view_name
REMOVE VIEW view_name
DROP VIEW view_name
DESTROY VIEW view_name
questions.answer: DROP VIEW view_name

questions.question: What does DROP VIEW IF EXISTS prevent?
options: Dropping a view that contains data
An error if the view does not exist
Accidentally dropping a table instead of a view
Other users from accessing the view
questions.answer: An error if the view does not exist

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid use case for a view?
options: Permanently storing calculated columns on disk
Simplifying complex queries by saving them as a reusable virtual table
Increasing the storage capacity of the database
Automatically backing up data every hour
questions.answer: Simplifying complex queries by saving them as a reusable virtual table

questions.question:
In the query below, what type of subquery is used?
SELECT name FROM students s1 WHERE gpa > (SELECT AVG(gpa) FROM students s2 WHERE s2.major = s1.major);
options: Scalar subquery
List subquery
Correlated subquery
Derived table
questions.answer: Correlated subquery

questions.question: What does NOT EXISTS return?
options: TRUE when the subquery returns at least one row
TRUE when the subquery returns no rows
The count of rows not matching
NULL if the subquery is empty
questions.answer: TRUE when the subquery returns no rows

questions.question: Which clause can you use on a view just like on a regular table?
options: ALTER COLUMN
ADD CONSTRAINT
WHERE
CREATE INDEX
questions.answer: WHERE

questions.question: What happens if a subquery used with a comparison operator (=, >, <) returns more than one row?
options: Only the first row is used
The query returns all matching rows
An error occurs
The results are automatically averaged
questions.answer: An error occurs

questions.question: Which of the following correctly uses a derived table?
options: SELECT * FROM (SELECT major, AVG(gpa) AS avg_gpa FROM students GROUP BY major) AS stats;
SELECT * FROM SELECT major, AVG(gpa) FROM students GROUP BY major;
SELECT * INTO (SELECT major, AVG(gpa) FROM students GROUP BY major);
SELECT * FROM DERIVED (SELECT major FROM students);
questions.answer: SELECT * FROM (SELECT major, AVG(gpa) AS avg_gpa FROM students GROUP BY major) AS stats;

questions.question: Why might you prefer EXISTS over IN when checking for matching rows in a large table?
options: EXISTS always returns fewer rows
EXISTS can stop as soon as it finds one matching row, which can be faster
IN does not work with subqueries
EXISTS supports more data types than IN
questions.answer: EXISTS can stop as soon as it finds one matching row, which can be faster
